content:
PLANT OPERATING MANUAL
Plant No. 140,170 Plant Name: POWER PLANT-2
Chapter No: 1 Rev. No. 2 Rev. Date : 10-07-2021
ADMINISTRATIVE REQUIREMENTS
Visakh Refinery
SECTION - A
PREFACE
The principal objective of the operating manual is to describe relevant operating procedures,
instructions and process safety information in an orderly manner for use by operating
personnel for safe and efficient operation of the plant. These operating procedures and
instructions are up-to-date reflecting changes in plant hardware and operating practices
carried out from time to time.
Power Plant-2 (PP-2) unit consists FCCU-2 CO Boiler, FCCU-1 CO Boiler, WHRS
(Waste Heat Recovery Steam Generator. VREP-I Pressure Reducing and De-superheating
(PRDS), VREP-II PRDS, VREP-I Bearing cooling water (BCW) System and VREP-II
BCW System.
FCCU-2 CO Boiler, VREP-I PRDS and VREP-I BCW system were commissioned in the
year 1985 under the Visakh Refinery Expansion Project-I (VREP-I) and FCCU-I CO Boiler
and VREP-II PRDS and VREP-II BCW System were commissioned in the year 1999 under
Visakh Refinery Expansion Project-II (VREP-II). WHRS commissioned in year 2018.
The present edition of the operating manual of Power Plant-2 is the revised version of earlier
operating manual prepared on 03-11-2015. The operating procedures are prepared / updated
basis the inputs available in the process Safety Information, Plant Standing Instructions
(PSI) issued and the past operating experiences.
Primary purpose of this revised operating manual is to consolidate, improve and upgrade all
the operating procedures fulfilling the requirements of Process Safety Management (PSM)
standard. HPCL Visakh Refinery adopted OSHA (Occupational Safety & Health
Administration) PSM Standard 29-CFR-1910.119 (PSM for highly hazardous chemicals) in
2013 and released the “PSM Procedure for preparation of Operating Procedures” (PSM-PR-
4) in the refinery. The present version of the Power Plant-2 Operating Manual is prepared
as per PSM-PR-4 and PSM audit observations in order to make the Operating Manual
OSHA PSM Standard compliant.
Operating procedures & conditions given in this manual are indicative and general guide for
routine start-up and operation of the unit. Vendor’s manual can be referred for detailed
operating instructions and precautions. Efforts have been made to include the relevant
information in a concise, step-by-step, easy-to-read format so that they are within the
comprehension of the readers. The users of this manual are encouraged to read the operating
manual and provide suggestions for further improvements of the manual.
Signature
Date 19-08-2021
Name Ch Ratnakara Rao
CGM – Commissioning
Designation
Department - Operations

SECTION - C
RECORDS OF REVISION, VALIDATION AND PLANT STANDING
INSTRUCTIONS
The Operating Procedures and Practices w.r.to unit start up, normal operation, non-routine
operations, normal shutdown and emergency shutdown are periodically reviewed for
improvements based on experiences, new requirements and better understanding of the process.
Accordingly, operating manuals are validated every year and revised once in 5 years.
The yearly validation and revision details of the manual are as below:
 The present edition of unit operating manual is reviewed and validated for the current
operation of the plant as indicated in the Table-1 below.
 A record of Plant Standing Instructions (PSI) issued in the intervening period is listed in
Table-2.
 The PSIs issued since last revision till the date of next revision (after 5 years) of the
operating manual is reviewed for its applicability in the current operation during yearly
validation and the same is indicated during validation. During revision of the manual after
5 years, all the applicable PSIs are properly integrated in the revised manual.

SECTION - E
PROCEDURE FOR REVISION OF THE OPERATING MANUAL
1. This Operating Manual is revised for the following :
 Change in Operating practice in any part of the Plant
 Implementation of changes in Hardware and/or software systems of the Plant which
have impact on operating procedure.
 Change in Chemicals.
 Changes in Safety systems.
2. The revision of the Operating Manual is done in two stages :
 Managing changes in the Operating Manual within 5 year cycle by validating the
manual annually.
 Updating (revising) the manual after 5 years of earlier revision.
3. Changes in any operating procedure are issued as “Plant Standing Instructions,” which
is maintained in a separate file by respective Division Head - Operations. Copy of the
valid Standing Instructions is made available in plant field room also for reference. A
record of applicable Plant Standing Instructions is maintained in Section-C of Chapter
1 in the Operating Manual controlled copies.
4. The Plant Standing Instructions are appropriately incorporated in the Operating Manual
during next revision of the Manual.

content:
PLANT OPERATING MANUAL
Plant No. 140,170 Plant Name: POWER PLANT-2
Chapter No: 2 Rev. No. 2 Rev. Date : 10-07-2021
INTRODUCTION
Visakh Refinery
CHAPTER - 2
INTRODUCTION
The steam is required in the refinery for the following Purposes:
i. For Heating purpose
ii. For Driving Turbines which drives Pumps & Blowers
iii. For atomising fuel oil in FO Burner
iv. For producing vacuum in the column.
v. For cleaning purpose.
VHP, HP and MP steams are required for driving turbines. MP/LP steam is required for
heating and steaming. MP steam is used for atomisation and used as motive fluid for vacuum
ejectors.
Bearing Cooling water is required for cooling the bearings of pumps and turbines in units.
The Power Plant-2 mainly consists of the following Equipment
i. Boilers (FCCU-2 CO, WHRS & FCCU-1 CO Boilers).
ii. VREP-I & II Pressure Reducing and De-super heating systems (PRDS).
iii. VREP-I & II Bearing Cooling Water (BCW) Systems.
FCCU-2 CO Boiler and FCCU-1 CO Boilers generates VHP Steam. FCCU-2 CO Boiler
was designed, supplied, erected by Heat Research Corporation Ltd, USA. FCCU-1 CO
Boiler was designed, supplied and erected by (Bharat Heavy Plates and Vessels Limited
(BHPV) (Presently BHEL), Visakhapatnam.
WHRS (Waste Heat Recovery Steam Generator) is provided between FCCU-II CO Boiler
and FGD-II to enable recovery of heat from hot flue gases at FCCU-II CO Boiler outlet to
FGD-II. It generates LP Steam.
Hot flue gases from FCCU-II routed to FCCU-II CO Boiler through orifice chamber for
steam generation and the hot flue gases, downstream of the Boiler at 320 deg C, routed to
FGD-II for SPM and SOx removal. Hence, it was proposed to provide WHRS to generate
steam by recovering heat from flue gases. In this process, flue gas temperature at the inlet
of FGD-II will reduce from 320 deg C to 200 deg C.
VREP-I&VREP-II PRDS Coverts VHP steam into MP Steam and MP steam into LP steam
as per process demand. VREP-I & II PRDS Systems designed by EIL and erected by HPCL
Projects Dept.
VREP-I BCW system supplies BCW to all VREP-I units and VREP-II BCW supplies BCW
to VREP-II units. VREP-I &II BCW Systems Designed by EIL and erected by HPCL
Projects Dept.
Prepared by eviewed by Approved by
Signature with
date
Name K Sai Ranjit Kumar K Rajasekhar Nirav Kumar Verma
Assistant Manager - DGM - Opns GM - Oprns

content:
3.0. Process Design Basis:
FCCU-2 CO Boiler is a VHP steam Boiler designed for VREP-I steam requirement.
It is designed to burn the FCCU-2 CO gas also. It is a 60 T/hr, 35.5 kg/cm2 and 370
deg C. it is commissioned in 1984.
After the revamp of FCCU-2 unit in 2010, the lean CO gas fuel from FCCU-2 unit
has reduced the CO Boiler steam generating capacity from 60 t/hr to 40 T/hr along
with supplementary fuel gas firing.
FCCU-1 CO Boiler is a VHP steam Boiler designed for VREP-2 steam requirement.
It is designed to burn the FCCU-1 CO gas also. It is a 40 T/hr, 35.5 kg/cm2 and 370
deg C. it is commissioned in 1999.
WHRS Steam drum outlet pressure maintained at 12.5 Kg/cm2 (g) and Steam drum
outlet steam temperature: 185 Deg. C and LP steam generating capacity id 6.1 TPH
VREP-I & II PRDS Systems reduces pressure from 35.5 to 10 kg/cm2 and 10 to
3.5kg/cm2 and de-superheat the steam by injection of BFW Water at respective
pressures.
VREP-I BCW Systems Circulating Capacity is 250 m3/hr at 7.5 kg/cm2, It is
Induced draft cross flow type, operating Inlet temperature is 44 deg C and Outlet
Temperature is 34 deg C.
VREP-II BCW Systems Circulating Capacity is 400 m3/hr at 7.5 kg/cm2,, It is
Induced draft cross flow type, operating Inlet temperature is 44 deg C and Outlet
Temperature is 33 deg C.

FCCU-II & FCCU-I CO Boilers are dual fired (Oil & Gas) Boilers. The unique feature
of CO Boilers is that CO Gas Coming from FCCU Process will be routed stack through
CO Boilers where Heat in CO gas utilized for generating steam. The saturation

Hot flue gases from FCCU-II routed to OCO boiler through orifice chamber for steam
generation and the hot flue gases, downstream of the boiler at 320 deg C, are routed to
FGD-II for SPM and SOx removal.
It was observed that 6.0 TPH of LP steam can be generated by recovering the heat from
hot flue gases. Hence, it was proposed to provide Waste Heat Recovery System to
generate steam by recovering heat from flue gases. In this process, flue gas temperature
at the inlet of FGD-II will reduce from 320 deg C to 200 deg C.

to Units.

content:
6.1. Brief Process Description of Boilers:
FCCU-2 CO Boiler is a gas and oil fired boiler erected in the year 1985 in VREP-1
project. Initially it is designed for 60 t/hr. But after the revamp of FCCU-2 unit in 2010
the lean CO gas fuel from FCCU-2 unit has reduced the CO Boiler steam generating
capacity from 60 t/hr to 40 T/hr along with supplementary fuel gas firing. As it is VHP
steam generation the pressure and temperature of steam are 36 kg/cm 2 and 360 Deg C.
Fuel gas is the auxiliary fuel fire provided in this boiler.
FCCU-1 CO Boiler is a CO gas fired boiler erected in the year 1999 in VREP-2 project.
It is designed for 40 T/hr, 36 kg/cm2 and 360 Deg C. Fuel oil/ Fuel gas are the auxiliary
fuels fired in this boiler.
6.2. Brief Process Description of WHRS:
WHRS is designed for dry run conditions. There is no bypass facility for the WHRS.
FCCU-II CO Boiler flue gas outlet routed to WHRS instead of FGD-II. WHRS flue gas
outlet routed to FGD-II for treatment. Sulphur dew point was calculated as 175 deg C for
the design composition of the flue gas. Therefore, in order to avoid acid dew point
corrosion, the evaporator coil operating temperature has to be at least 10 deg C above
Sulphur dew point. Accordingly, it was envisaged that the boiler is to be operated at 10.5
kg/cm2g and saturation temperature of 185 deg C. The same is to be let down to 3.0/3.5
kg/cm2g using a Pressure Control Valve.LP steam generated from the system is routed
to LP steam header at the FCCU-II CO Boiler FD fan turbine discharge. VHP BFW for
the new system is taken from OCO boiler VHP BFW provision at the upstream of BFW
control valve. All other utilities requirement for the new system are taken from FCCU-II
battery limit.
6.3. Brief Process Description of PRDS:
There are Two Types of PRDS in PP-2 they are 1) VREP-I PRDS 2) VREP-II PRDS.
VREP-I PRDS (2Nos) Reduces VHP Steam (35.5 Kg/cm2) to MP Steam (10.5 kg/cm2)
and VLP Steam (3.5 kg/cm2)
VREP-II PRDS (2Nos) Reduces VHP Steam (35.5Kg/cm2) to MP Steam (10.5Kg/cm2)
and MP Steam (10.5Kg/cm2) to LP Steam (3.5Kg/cm2).
6.4. Brief Process Description of BCW System:
This BCW system consists of two unit’s viz., BCW VREP-1 and BCW VREP-2. These
units supply Bearing Cooling Water at desired pressure and temperature to VREP-1 and
VREP-2 units. BCW VREP-1 system which is commissioned in the year 1985 supplies
BCW to all VREP-1 units and some off-site location. BCW VREP-2 which is
commissioned in the year 1999 supplies BCW to VREP-2 units, Sea water Cooling tower
pumps and few off site pumps. This cooling water is used for bearing housing cooling,
pump gland cooling, turbine oil coolers and air compressor coolers etc., where salt water
cannot be used. The cooling water supply at desired pressure, temperature and quality is
very much required for efficient heat exchange. Failure of which cause damage to
bearings and other rotating parts, tripping of unit and sometimes cease of shaft.
VREP-1 BCW system:
This system consists of
1) Cooling water tower with two cells
2) Sump
3) Recirculation pumps
4) Chemical dosing facility
5) Supply and Return header.
6) Make up water facility

FCCU-2 CO BOILER CONTROL LOOPS
The CO Boiler is designed for combusting the FCCU - II regenerator off-gas containing
lean concentrations of carbon monoxide in the presence of auxiliary fuel oil and/or fuel
gas. The heat from the combustion is used to heat feed water and generate superheated
steam.
The CO bearing flue gas is burned in the presence of auxiliary fuel to provide a maximum
continuous rated production of 40 t/hr of steam (after FCCU-II revamp) at a gauge
pressure of 38 kg/cm2 and a temperature of 370C.
The boiler consists of two major components. The first is the hot refractory line
combustor, in which complete incineration of carbon monoxide takes place, supported
by firing of auxiliary chamber at several points in the arch above the supplementary
burners. The second component is the horizontal tube convection section in which heat
recovery takes place.
The hot refractory line combustor is designed for temperatures in the range of 1000 0 C or
higher, to ensure complete conversion of the carbon monoxide in the Regenerator off gas
and to maintain stable ignition during the transient effects accompanying load changes
or varying rates of coke burn. The firebox size ensures sufficient combustion residence
time; so that the entire CO is completely converted to CO2 before any significant
temperature reduction is permitted to take place as a result of heat transfer to cold
absorbing surfaces. In effect, the ideal adiabatic conversion is approached.
The steam generation section is based on a natural circulation system utilizing a
conservative circulation ratio which provides inlet velocities to the steam generation coil
in excess of 2 meters per second. Tabulators are provided in bottom two rows of the
lower steam generator coil. These swirlers increase the turbulence and mixing action of
the water steam mixture thereby improving the fluid flow and heat transfer characteristics
of the tubes exposed to the highest flue gas temperature in addition to the inlet manifolds
are sized and individual risers are provided to assure uniform flow to each pass. The tube
coils are designed using 114 mm OD tubes with 5 mm wall thickness which is well in
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 33 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 HVGO PUMPS > FCCU-2 CO BOILER CONTROL LOOPS

content:
excess of actual code requirements, representing a substantial safety factor. The use of
this tube size and thickness presents three advantages. First, large tubes minimize any
problems resulting from high concentration of solids in the boiler water. Second, extra
wall thickness gives added protection should any problem of external erosion develop.
Third possibility of tube vibration is eliminated, which is sometimes a problem with tubes
of smaller diameter. Any tube in the entire boiler system can be removed and replaced
individually without the need for removing or even forcing aside any other tube. Header
boxes are provided at both ends of the convection section.
The unit is designed for high flue gas velocities which help maintain “clean” convection
surface. These velocities maintain a check on solids build up on heat transfer surface but
without creating problems of tube erosion. Three levels of two retractable soot blowers
complete with a scavenging and sealing air system are provided.
The side wall and floors of the combustor are of double wall construction with a forced
flow of combustion air. This design eliminates combustor heat losses. Combustion air is
provided by the turbine and motor driven forced draft fan. Each fan is provided with an
inlet air silencer and connecting duct with isolation dampers.
6.4.2 FCCU-I CO Boiler:
The schematic diagram of FCCU-I CO Boiler is shown below.

The CO-furnace is a cylindrical vessel of 4m ID (Net) x 9.5m length (TL-TL) attached
horizontally to the CO-Boiler, such that the combustion products directly enter the boiler.
The furnace is internally lined with two-layers of refractories viz. Rescocast AA 22S
925 mm THK), with SS 310 Hexmesh and insulating castable type-V of EIL spec. 6-79-
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 34 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 HVGO PUMPS > FCCU-2 CO BOILER CONTROL LOOPS > 6.4.2 FCCU-I CO Boiler:

content:
08 temperature. Outer surface of furnace shell is provided with no insulation but with
protective hood on its top. The protective hood is provided with chimneys (with cover)
to maintain proper air circulation and also it acts as a rain hood. The furnace is equipped
with three numbers of CO-burners and one number of support burner. Co-burners are
provided on the circular shell of the CO-furnace, firing tangentially to make a whirlpool,
thereby increasing the CO-gas. Support burner is also suitable for firing of LSHS & fuel
oil and provided with a pilot burner firing fuel gas continuously at all cases of operation
of the boiler. The furnace supports are heated by saturated water from steam drum to
minimize thermal stresses in the system. The maximum temperature of the furnace is
maintained around 1000 0 C, by controlling the support burner firing duty and combustion
air flow to CO-furnace.
The CO-Boiler is a vertical, water tube boiler with membrane wall type construction. It
has a rectangular cross section and operates on natural circulation principle. The
membrane wall evaporative shell houses the superheater and evaporator (Fahnen) tube
bundles. Economizer tubes are provided above the Fahnen tubes in metallic shell casing.
Flue gas from the boiler at 2450C (max) is further cooled down to 1800` 1850C in a cast
iron gilled ring economizer, located downstream of the CO-Boiler. Flue gasses from the
gilled ring economizer exit to atmosphere at 60m elevation through the grade mounted
stack. The boiler stack is of metal plate (CS) construction and internally lined with 19mm
of low cement castable (50% Al2, O3 min, 0.8% Fe2, O3 max, 2% CaO max) on hot face
with SS304H hexmesh followed by 65 mm thick insulating castable (type-II of EIL spec.
6-79-08). No external insulation is provided on stack. However, stack metallic shell is
provided with 3 coats of heat resisting chimney black paint internally.
The Boiler membrane walls are provided with 105 mm thick mineral wool insulation
with Alcladding (for protection against weather) externally to minimize the heat loss to
atmosphere. The economizer section is provided with 155 mm thick mineral wool
insulation externally and the downstream flue gas ducting up to the exit of gilled ring
economizer is provided with 105 mm thick mineral wool insulation externally. Flue gas
ducting d/s of Gilled ring economizer up to the stack is to be applied with 40 mm thick
mineral wool insulation for personnel protection, complete flue gas ducting from CO-
Boiler to stack to be applied with 19 mm thick Rescocast AA 22 (s) lining internally with
SS304H Hexmesh to prevent erosion.
The boiler is provided with a combustion chamber for auxiliary fuel firing, which is made
by water-wall tubes. All the water walls of auxiliary combustion chamber are provided
with separate Risers and Distributors for water circulation and all the water circuit
operate on natural circulation principle. The bottom wall of auxiliary firing chamber is
lined with a layer of refractory bricks (type-1 of EIL specn. 6-79-03).
Three number of top down shot fired, low Nox, combination fuel fired burners of total
capacity of 31.5 x 100 kcal/hr to fire fuel gas/LSHS/Fuel oil are installed on top wall of
the auxiliary firing combustion chamber. Each auxiliary burner is provided with a fuel
gas fired pilot burner for lighting the main burners. The combustion products enter the
radiation chamber of boiler at bottom.
The super heater tube bundles are located just on top of the radiation chamber and
followed by FAHNEN I and then by FAHNEN II (1st and 2nd evaporative tube bundles)
respectively. FAHNEN I consists of 16 numbers of tube rows i.e. 4 numbers of tubes
branching out from each water wall tube of wall No.3 and again joining back to the
respective mother wall tubes. FAHNEN II is identical to that of FAHNEN I, but branches
out from the opposite water wall (i.e. Wall No. 4) instead. Membrane wall terminates at
the boiler top headers which are connected to the steam drum through riser pipes.
Economizer tube bundle is housed in a metallic casing above the top Boiler headers.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 35 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 HVGO PUMPS > FCCU-2 CO BOILER CONTROL LOOPS > 6.4.2 FCCU-I CO Boiler:

content:
Gases coming out from the economizer, passes through the gilled ring economizer and
finally vented to atmosphere at 60m elevation routing through downstream ducting and
grade mounted stack.
The boiler system is provided with one number of steam drum of size 1500 mm I/D and
7000 mm TL to TL length at a C/L elevation of 24.9 m (i.e. Just above the economizer
elevation), operating at a pressure of 43 kg/cm2 (g). Externally mounted boiler feed
water preheater is provided to preheat BFW to 1650C (coming out from gilled ring
economizer) before entering into economizer to avoid acid dew point corrosion in the
cold end of economizer tube bundle. Saturated steam condensate returns to the steam
drum by gravity. The acid dew point of flue gas in CO-mode and full fuel oil firing mode
BFW preheating can be limited to 1400C, since the acid dew point of flue gas is 1340C
(max).
The boiler is provided with 12 numbers of retractable type soot blowers at different
elevation with 2 nos. of soot blowers at each plane. Additionally, two nos retractable
type soot blowers are provided in gilled ring economizer. The soot blowers are motor
driven and provided with automatic relay logic sequential control system. Soot blowing
medium is superheated MP steam. The boiler system is provided with one set of forced
draft fan system common to both auxiliary fuel firing & CO-combustion. Normally
working FD fan is motor driven, whereas the standby one is steam turbine driven. FD
fan system also provides sealing air for all soot blowers, cooling air for idle burners and
cooling air for flame scanners. Seal air fans are provided to supply sealing air for
guillotine dampers at inlet CO-duct and boiler exhaust duct, to achieve 100% sealing. In
addition, one more set of seal air fans (1w+1s) are provided to supply seal air to FD fan
discharge dampers to prevent CO gas leaking to atmosphere in the event of boiler
tripping.
CO-gas ducting (from TPSV to CO-furnace) material is CS and is provided with a layer
of Resco cast 17 EMC (125 mm thick) with SS 304H Hexmesh. CO-gas ducting to be
provided with no external insulation. Refractory lining in CO-gas ducting downstream
of TPSV shall be in line with the same on upstream of the TPSV.
4 Nos. Of sample cooler are provided for sampling of BFW, Boiler water, saturated steam
and superheated steam.
A wet gas scrubber will be installed on flue gas circuit at downstream of CO-Boiler
system (i.e. after gilled ring economizer) to remove oxides of sulphur (SO2 & SO3) from
flue gas before venting it to atmosphere through a separate stack. CO-Boiler system is
designed for operation with & without the wet gas scrubber on line. For online operation
of wet gas scrubbers, flue gas pressure shall be 300 mm WCG at inlet of wet gas scrubber
at maximum flow rate of the gas (i.e. 111666 kg/hr at Case-VI operation). At other
operating cases, flue gas pressure at inlet of scrubber shall be reduced accordingly (i.e.
For case I & II operation, flue gas pressure shall be 82 mm WCG at inlet of scrubber).
6.6. Detailed Process Description of WHRS:
It is proposed to recover heat from the hot flue gases of FCCU-II CO boiler before routing
to FGD-II by providing a new waste heat boiler.
The following fig shows the water and steam flow in the WHRS

LP STEAM
BFW
The design exit temperature of the hot flue gases from FCCU-II CO boiler is 320 degC.
This high stack temperature is due to inadequacy of the existing heat transfer area for the
revamped conditions of FCCU-II. Around 6 MT of steam can be generated using the
waste heat from the flue gases which will lower the temperature of the flue gases to
around 200 degC before routing to FGD-II.
Further, reduction in flue gas temperature will reduce service water make up rate in FGD-
II due to reduced evaporation losses.
6.7. Detailed Process Description of PRDS:
There are Two Types of PRDS (Pressure Reducing and De-Superheating) in PP-2 they
are 1) VHP-MP PRDS & 2) MP-LP PRDS.
VHP-MP PRDS System:
VHP steam is reduced to MP level of 10.5 Kg/cm2 pressure in a pressure reducing station
140-PIC-301 operating on the control provided downstream of the control valves and is
set to relieve at 12.5 KG/cm2 pressure. MP steam is desuperheated from 354 0 C to 250
0 C in a mechanical spray type desuperheater 140-X-03 by spraying MP BFW. 140-TIC-
301 controls the temperature at 250 0 C by regulating the control valve 140-V-301
provided on the BFW line. 140-TAH-301 sounds high temperature alarm at 270 0 C and
140-TAL-302 sounds low temperature alarm at 245 0 C of the MP steam downstream of
desuperheater. 140-PAL-302 gives low pressure alarm of MP steam at 10.0 Kg/cm2. MP
steam generated at HRSG - I & HRSG - II joins to PP-II MP header through a 14” line.
The same line also joins to PP-I MP steam header.
The MP steam generated from HRSG - III and HRSG - IV is routed via 20” line to PP-
II. It is interconnected with 12” MP steam header with 12” connection. Under VREP-II
project one more reducing station provided to reduce HP steam to MP steam to meet the
requirement. PV-2301 is utilized to reduce the steam through controller PIC-2301 at
DCS. A 12” tap-off is taken from new VHP header coming from FCCU-(R) and after
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 37 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 HVGO PUMPS > FCCU-2 CO BOILER CONTROL LOOPS > 6.4.2 FCCU-I CO Boiler:

content:
reduction connected to new MP steam line from CPP through a 16” line. A desuperheater
140-X-09 is provided in the downstream of control valve to reduce temperature 360 to
250 0 C. Max allowable flow through 140-X-09 is 45 t/hr. Temperature is controlled by
TIC 2301. Safety valves PSV - 2301 / 2302 are provided after reducing station with a set
pressure 12.5 kg/cm2. MP steam is also produced in the steam generators of CDU - II,
CDU - III and FCCU - II. Exhaust of HP BFW pump is also MP steam.
MP steam is distributed to the following units:

To PP-I through 8” header, 140-FR/FQ-302 records and integrates the MP steam
flow rate exported. The normal export rate is 10-15 t/hr. But the interconnection is
designed for a flow rate of 20 t/hr.
 To High Lift Pump House through 10” header

To FCCU - II unit through 12” header

To CDU - II through 12” header
 To flare through 6” header
 To Bitumen storage and drum filling facilities through 3” header.

To CDU - III

To DHDS
MP-LP PRDS System:
LP steam is mainly produced at the exhaust of steam turbines. At PP-II Boiler FD fans
and MP BFW pump turbine exhaust producing LP steam. Further requirement of LP
steam is produced by reducing MP steam to LP steam level in pressure reducing station.
At the pressure reducing station, LP steam pressure is controlled by split range pressure
controller 140-PIC-302 acting on pressure control valves 140-PV-302A/A’/B. When LP
steam header pressure goes below the set point of 3.5 Kg/cm2, control valves 140-PV-
302 A/A’ will be regulated and 140-PV-302B will remain close. When LP steam header
pressure goes above the set point, then 140-PV-302A/A’ regulated to maintain header
pressure. After complete closing of control vakve140-PV-302 A/A’ if the LP header
pressure still remain in the higher side, 140-PV-302 opens and LP steam vented to
atmosphere to control the pressure. Pressure safety valve 140-PSV-302, set to relieve at
5.5 Kg/cm2 pressure, is provided downstream of pressure control valves 140-PV-302
A/A’.
Steam is desuperheated from 250 0 C to 160 0 C in mechanical spray type Desuperheater
140-X-04 by spraying MP BFW. The temperature is maintained at 160 0 C by 140-TIC-
302 located atleast 4 meter away from the downstream of desuperheater by regulating
the control valve 140-TV-302 located on the BFW line. 140-TAH-303 sounds pressure
alarm of LP steam header at 3.2 Kg/cm2. LP steam produced in steam generators of units
and exhaust of IFO pump turbine also joins the LP steam header.
To meet VREP - II & DHDS requirement, one more reducing station is provided to
reduce MP steam to LP steam. One tap-off is taken from new MP header from HRSG III
& IV and reduced through PV-2303 with PIC-2303 in DCS. One attemperator 140-X-10
(with TIC 2303) is provided in the reduced 20” line to reduce temperature from 250 0 C
to 155 0 C. Maximum Flow allowable through the attemperator is 30T/hr. Safety valves
PSV-2303/2304/2305 are provided after reducing station with a set pressure 5.5 Kg/cm2.
LP steam is distributed to the following consumers:

To Deaerators

To fuel oil tank farm

To FCCU – II

To CDU – II

6.8. Detailed Process Description of BCW System:
VREP-I BCW System:
Bearing cooling water requirement is met by four pumps 170-PM-5A/B/C and 170/24-
PM-2G2. 170-PM-5A/C are of capacity 350 m3/hr at 6 kg/cm2 pressure and 170/24-PM-
2G2 is of capacity 97 m3/hr at 6.5 kg/cm2 pressure take suction from the sump and
discharge into a 8 inch supply header. This header supplies to all the units. The 8 inch
supply header is provided with a pressure gauge, Temperature Indicator, Pressure
transmitter and flow transmitter. Also a low pressure alarm provided, which sounds at
MOI PP panel when low pressure activates.
There is one more pump 170-PM-5B is available of capacity 275 m3/hr at 3.5 kg/cm2
pressure. This pump can not be put in service due to low discharge pressure (shut off
pressure 4.5 kg/cm2).
Normally two pumps one of 170-PM-5A/C with combination of 17024-PM-2G2 will be
in service and the second one is available as standby.
BCW after serving its purpose, return to cooling tower through a 8 inch header. One 6
inch line from Merox joins to 8 inch header near the battery limit. One more 6 inch return
header from CDU-2 joined to the return header near the tower. A 2 inch tapping provided
in return header for blowdown. The return header provided with a sample point and
temperature indicator.
The hot water from various units is cooled in the induced draft cross flow tower. The
tower is designed for a maximum circulating rate 250m3/hr. It has two cells each of
capacity 125 m3/hr. The hot water from return header distributed to both the shells either
side. The distribution of water can be adjusted by the help of butterfly valves (4nos).
The water then distributed through distributing nozzles and falls across the path of air
flow. Packings are provided inside the tower for braking of water particles and increase
the contact time with air for greater cooling. The cooled water is then collected in cooling
tower basin. The basin has two compartments each receiving water from respective
shells. Water from these compartments flows via two channels to BCW sump. A screen
is installed at the inlet of suction of pump for preventing entry of foreign materials to
pump. The suction sump is having over flow and draining facility. The sump is provided
with high and low level alarms, which sound at MOI PP panel . The sump also provided
with a level controller, which controls makeup water flow depending on level.
To supplement the loss of cooling water due to leak, blowdown, evaporation and drift
through cooling tower, raw water is supplied through 2 inch line by raw water pumps
170-PM-02A/B/D/E situated at DM-2. Water flow is controlled by control valve, which
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 39 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 HVGO PUMPS > 6.8 Detailed Process Description of BCW System:

content:
get input from sump level controller. By-pass facility of control valve is also available.
In case of failure of make-up water supply, raw water which is stored for CRP filter back
wash in the Back wash raw water tank can be routed. Also DM water can be used for
makeup through filter back wash lines. Usually 80-90% sump level is maintained.
VREP-II BCW System:
VREP-2 Bearing cooling water requirement is met by two pumps 170-PM-9A/B. 170-
PM-9A/B of capacity 400 m3/hr at 5.5 kg/cm2 pressure take suction from the sump and
discharge into a 12 inch supply header. From the supply header one 3 inch tap-off is
taken for FCCU feed pump and Reciculating cooling water tower. Another 10 inch tap-
off taken for Merox unit and air compressor. There is a 6 inch tie-in between VREP-1
and VREP-2 BCW supply at Merox unit. After the take of for Merox block the 12 inch
header reduced to 10 inch and supply water to CDU-3. From this 10 inch header one 2
inch tap-off is taken to cater to VBU feed pump near DM plant. The supply header is
provided with a pressure gauge, Temperature Indicator, Pressure transmitter and flow
transmitter. Also a low pressure alarm provided, which sounds at MOI PP panel when
low pressure activates. Auto cut-in facility of spare pump is also available. At 4.5kg/cm2
auto cut-in of spare pumps takes place.
BCW returns form all consumer units as same size of supply headers are connected to a
12 inch header which feeds warm water to cooling tower top. One 3 inch tapping
provided in return header for Side stream filter 170-X-09 of capacity 8 m3/hr. It filters
suspended solids 60 ppm at inlet to 10 ppm at outlet. Feed line to filter is provided with
a flow indicator. One DP indicator is provided across the filter bed. Max allowable DP
is 0.5 kg/cm2. After filtration, this water returns to cooling water sump. Back flushing
facility is provided for the Side stream filter. This is to be back washed once in a day or
if DP across the filter crosses 0.5 kg/cm2 which ever comes earlier. The return header
provided with a pressure gauge, Temperature gauge and temperature indicator. One 8
inch tapping provided from return header for blow down. Blow down water is routed to
surface drain.
The hot water from the various units is cooled in the induced draft cross flow tower. The
tower is designed for a maximum circulating rate 400m3/hr. It has two cells each of
capacity 200 m3/hr. The hot water from return header distributed to both shells on either
side. The distribution of water can be adjusted by help of butterfly valves on individual
distribution line. The water then distributed through distributing nozzles and fall across
the path of air flow. Packing are provided inside the tower for braking of water particle
and increase the contact time with air for greater cooling. The cooled water then collected
in cooling tower basin. The basin has two compartments each receiving water from
respective shell. Water from these compartment flows via two channels to BCW sump.
A double screen is installed at the channel near inlet of sump for preventing entry of
foreign materials to the pump suction. The basins are provided with isolating gate. Each
basin is provided with drain. The suction sump is having over flow and draining facility.
The drain valve operated to remove mud and slim deposit. BCW pumps take suction
from the sump. Suction strainers are provided inside the sump. The sump is provided
with high and low level alarms, which sound at MOI PP panel . The sump also provided
with a level controller, which controls make-up water flow depending on level.
To supplement the loss of cooling water due to leak, blowdown, evaporation and drift
through cooling tower, raw water is supplied through 4 inch line by raw water pump 170-
PM-02A/B situated at DM-2. One flow meter is provided on the makeup water line to
know the makeup. Water flow is controlled by control valve, which gets input from sump
level controller. By-pass facility of control valve also available. In case of failure of
make-up water supply, raw water which is stored for CRP filter back wash in the Back
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:6 Rev. No.2 Page 40 of 251

wash raw water tank can be routed. Also DM water can be used for makeup through filter
back wash water tank lines. Usually 80-90% sump level is maintained.

7.2. WHRS Equipment Details
The WHRS is a Single-drum bottom supported design with provision for free expansion
upwards. Boiler structure & Bottom supporting transmits all loads to the boiler foundation.
Major boiler components can be defined as under:
STEAM DRUM:
The drum is made of CS plate, cold rolled to a circular shape and fitted with dished ends
and manholes to allow easy accessibility for maintenance. Holes are drilled in the drum for
connecting down-comer / riser tubes for maintaining natural circulation. Steam separator
& baffles made of MS plates are fitted inside the drum, to separate the steam from water
and dry the same. Connections are also provided to check water level through gauge glass
and control the level through automatic controllers. Nozzles / stubs are provided to mount
safety valve, vent valve, pressure & temperature gauges.
EVAPORATOR:
Coil type tubular Evaporator, connect with inlet & outlet headers consisting of 38.1 OD x
5 mm THK tube provided. Evaporator inlet header connected to steam drum thru down
comer and top header is also connected to steam drum thru riser. This boiler has a single
evaporator. Evaporator is enclosed 4 sides by Boiler casing with refractory application.
ECONOMISER
Coil type tubular economizer, consisting of 38.1 OD x 5 mm THK tube with inlet & outlet
headers. Economizer is enclosed 4 sides by Boiler casing with refractory application.

8.2. Functioning of (operating Mechanism) of the complicated control Schemes:
Boiler Drum level control (Three Element Drum level Control)
The boiler drum is located near the top of the boiler. Its main function is to provide
surface area and volume for separation of steam from water. The measure of water
quantity in a boiler is indicated by the level of the water steam interface in the drum.
A drum level control system is designed for a continuous mass balance. The liquid level
in the drum must be kept within a narrow range of operation. Too high a level causes
water and it’s dissolved solids to be carried over into the steam system. Too low
circulation functions and may even result in tube rupture (burn out) due to lack of cooling
water on the boiling surfaces.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:8 Rev. No.2 Page 63 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 5 LT-4104 CBD TANK > 8.2 Functioning of (operating Mechanism) of the complicated control Schemes: > Boiler Drum level control (Three Element Drum level Control)

content:
In a three element drum level control system, three variables, viz., steam flow, feed water
flow and drum level, influence the feed water valve position (ref Fig). Steam flow is
measured by the flow transmitter FT whose signal is transmitted to the feed water flow
computer FY1. Drum level is measured by the level transmitter LT1 and its signal is
transmitted to the drum level controller LIC 1, which produces the necessary corrective
signal to maintain drum level at its set point. The controller’s output is transmitted to the
feed water flow computer, which combines the signals from the two variables and sends
an output in the form of set point to the feed water flow control FC1. Feed water flow is
measured by the flow transmitter FT2 whose signal is transmitted to the feed water
controller FC1, which produces the necessary corrective action to main feed water flow
vis-a-vis its set point. An output signal is then transmitted from feed water flow
controller FC1 to the feed water control valve FCV2.

8.4. Trips/Interlocks Bypassing Procedure:
In Refinery operation, various systems/ critical equipment’s are equipment’s are
equipped with safety interlocks and trips as protection devices to avoid the system/
equipment landing into abnormal/ unintended operations.
All operating personnel to ensure that the safety interlocks/trips are always in service for
smooth operation and to ensure required protection for all the critical equipment systems
as intended
The following procedure to be followed for bypassing trip interlocks
1. All trips & interlocks should always be on line
2. If any particular trip / interlock is to be bypassed; forced for any maintenance work,
it should be with the concurrence of unit-in-charge (during dayshift or working day)
or Division Head . Online requisition for Trip bypass to be made through TIMS
(Trips and Interlock Management System) and the required entries in the trip
register should be made. In off day shift hours, RSM/ Unit in charge/division head
of operations are authorized to grant permission.
3. Any trip/ interlock not on line for more than 72hrs has to be intimated to GM-
Operations in the specified format (annexure-I)
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:8 Rev. No.2 Page 68 of 251

content:
4. Any trip/ interlock not on line for more than 7days, has to be intimated to GM-
Operations in the specified Format (Foramt-II).
5. For all trips/ logistics, Instrument maintenance (in consultation with operation)
should develop and implement suitable preventive maintenance checks at suitable
periodicity.

6. For all trip / logics, instrument maintenance ( in concurrence with operations) should
develop and implement suitable preventive maintenance checks at suitable periodicity
7. Procedure for bypassing the trip tag for handing over to instrumentation
1. In Boiler/Equipment operation the trip tags play a vital role in safe guarding the
Boiler/Equipment from excessive operating conditions like level, pressure,
temperature etc. For proper functioning of the trip tags PM checks as well as
calibration of the tags is to be done in a periodical time frame. Handing over the
tags for PM checks or for repairs without getting disturbance to the operation of the
boiler is important.
8. Procedure For Bypassing The Trip Tag For Handing Over To Instrumentation
a) Prior to bypassing any trip, clearance is to be taken from Unit Manager & inform the
same to RSM.
b) Inform the concerned operator regarding the activity prior to bypassing the trip tag.
c) Monitor the parameters and maintain the process manually without any process upset
& bypass the trip tag ensuring with the field operator & handover the trip tag to
instrumentation.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:8 Rev. No.2 Page 71 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 8.0 APPROVAL OF GM - (O)

content:
d) The trip tag bypassed is to be noted down in the trip bypass register.
e) If the trip tag bypass time exceeds 72 hrs and the tag is still in bypassed condition, it
has to be informed to General Manager (Operations) for approval.
9. Procedure For Handing Over Trip Related Instruments / Equipments For
Rotary/Electrical PM Checks
a) Prior to handing over the equipment to the maintenance (rotary/electrical), unit
Manager Clearance is required.
b) Ensure the Stand by equipment either rotary/electrical healthiness before releasing
the in service particular equipment.
c) Instruments related to instrumentation rectification will be taken up in forced
condition with prior permission from unit Manager. These instruments cannot be
released on PM checks basis.
If proper scheduling could not be done on the particular, day for releasing the equipment,
again rescheduling to be done to release the particular equipment ensuring the standby
equipment is healthy.

Alarm Sub Status: AOF (Alarm Output Off):
Normally when an alarm occurs:
 The tag mark changes color depending on the type of the alarm.
 Audible alarm is heard.
 The alarm status (HI/LO/HH/LL etc…) is displayed on the process alarm window.
 In AOF mode:
 The tag mark changes to dark blue color irrespective of the alarm.
 Audible alarm is put off.
 The alarm status is displayed only on the instrument faceplate.
 All the alarms status except IOP & OOP on the instrument are not displayed on
the process alarm window.

To put the instrument to AOF mode:
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:9 Rev. No.2 Page 77 of 251

 Select AOF icon. Confirm.

To bring the instrument back to normal mode:
 Go to the tuning window of the instrument.
 Select AOF icon once again. Confirm.

content:
12.0. Start-up procedure of plant after short shut down:
Short shutdowns in the plant are due to Boilers trip, BFW pumps trip or power failure.
The plant to be restarted as per procedures explained below.
12.1. Start-up procedure of Boilers after short shut down:
1. Carryout trip checks if shut down is more than 72 hors
2. Start the Boiler as procedure mentioned below.
3. Open all the drains of all headers (VHP, HP, MP & LP) so that all the steam line
should be free from condensate.
4. All the man holes and covers should be properly closed.
5. Keep HP - MP, Atomizing steam & MP-LP reducing station in close position.
6. Take export steam from Power Plant - I.
7. When dry steam will come through the drains of 400# 150# and 20# steam headers,
close the drain and put steam trap in service.
8. Open the valves of steam tracing line of FO line.
9. Establish fuel oil circulation.
10. Put FD fan in service.
11. Super heater inlet and outlet drain valve should be in open position.
12. Startup vent valve should be in open position.
13. Steam drum vent valve should in open position.
14. Clean the Burner and fix it properly with gasket.
15. All the relief valves must be on line.
16. All the instrument air valves should be in open position. Keep all the transmitters in
service.
17. Open the block valves before and after the oil and water control valves.
18. Fill up the Boiler and put Boiler gauge glasses in service.
19. Open atomizing main stop valve for individual Boiler.
20. Open main Igniter Gas stop valve.
21. Open main Fuel Gas stop valve.
22. Open Fuel oil recirculation valve.
23. Give Power supply to BMS panel.
24. Make sure that all the systems of BMS panel to be used are in working condition.
25. Make sure that all the trip system is working (by simulation).
26. Check the igniter.
27. Be sure that all burners are cleaned.
28. Start the Boiler through BMS. Put the pilot in service.
29. Put oil Burner in service.
30. Keep the Boiler in low firing position. To attain a pressure of 36 kg/cm2 from Cold
condition min. 5 hours should be given.
31. During the initial firing keep FW and oil control in manual control.
32. Keep on watching the water level in the gauge glass and maintain the level until the
boiler stabilizes.
33. Once Boiler is stabilized, change fuel oil & feed water control to auto mode.
34. When drum pressure reaches 2 kg/cm2, close drum vent and super heater inlet drain.
35. When the drum pressure reaches up to 36 kg/cm2, open the main stop valve.
36. When the Boilers come on line close super heater outlet drain valve and start up vent
valve
37. Adjust the flame pattern by adjusting Air resisters.
38. Put fuel gas in service if available.
39. Slowly open all reducing stations.
40. Allow all condensate to drain.
41. Close the drain valves when dry steam comes through the drain.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:12 Rev. No.2 Page 93 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 31 During the initial firing keep FW and oil control in manual control.

content:
42. After pressuring up of LP header, put Deaerater in service.
43. Change slowly feed water pump suction (suction from tank) to hot suction (suction
from DA storage tank).
12.2. Start-up of PRDS after short shut down:
1. Keep all block valve on PRDS in closed position
2. Slowly open the control valve bypass valve and block valves’ equalizing valves to
charge the PRDS
3. Once PRDS Headers charged ensure control valve in closed position and keep the
control valve in auto and set the PRDS Down pressures.
4. Slowly close the control valve’s by pass valve and observe control valve action if
control valve is not acted accordingly, maintain steam pressure manually by
operating bypass valve and follow with instrument maintenance for repair the control
valve.
5. If PRDS downstream pressure maintained with control valve keep it in service and
close bypass valve.
6. Keep Attemperator in service by keeping the manual valve in service.
12.3. Start-up of BCW Systems after short shut down:
1. Clean the BCW sump thoroughly and drain the water from sump.
2. Fill the BCW Sump with service water through make up water line, Keep chemical
dosing in service.
3. Keep BCW supply and return valves in open position at battery limit.
4. Open BCW pumps suction and discharge valves in open position and start the BCW
pump.
5. Maintain BCW water supply pressure to rated valve i.e around 6-7kg/cm2 by
throttling the butterfly valves provided at the top of BCW tower.
6. Start ID fans, run both the ID fans in service if required to maintain the BCW supply
temperature at 30-35 degC.
12.4. Start-up of FD fan after short shut down:
1. If Motor tripped due to power failure, start the turbine driven pump
2. To start Turbine driven pump, inform to MOI Engineer to manage steam demand.
3. Ensure pump suction and discharge valves & BCW valves in open position.
4. Drain condensate from turbine and slowly increase the speed of the turbine.
5. Ensure Lube oil pressure is greater than 1.0kg/cm2
6. Increase the turbine speed till Feed water pressure is increased.
12.5. Start-up of plant after total power failure occurred:
1. Inform Shift engineer Ensure who will inform Electrical substation Engineer for
restoration of power.
2. Close isolation valves on fuel oil & Fuel gas lines. Water lines and steam line at
battery limits
3. Isolate PRDS upstream valves.
4. Once power resumed, open battery limits
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:12 Rev. No.2 Page 94 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 6 Keep Attemperator in service by keeping the manual valve in service. > 12.5 Start-up of plant after total power failure occurred:

content:
5. Start BFW Pumps with recirculation open
6. Start Boilers as per procedure.
7. Keep PRDS in service.
12.6. WHRS Startup Boiler Startup Sequence after Short shutdown or Emergency
Shutdown:
1. All air vent and Start up vent partially open and Drain valve closed.
2. Water fills in the Boiler 50 percent of Steam Drum.
3. Start CO Boiler by closing new boiler inlet damper and open damper towards Chimney’
4. Open the Boiler inlet damper partially instead of slowly heat up boiler.
5. After riches 2 Kg/cm²(g) pressure all air vent closed except Startup vent.
6. After rise 5 Kg/cm²(g) crack leak Steam stop valve.
7. Steam line drain valve open.
8. After rise pressure 9 Kg/cm²(g) Steam stop valve to be open and start up vent and all
vent to be closed.
9. Then adjust – Boiler inlet damper & damper towards chimney.
10. Start Feed Water Pump and maintain Normal Water level of steam drum.
11. Every after 4 hours 1-minute main blow down valve should be opened (Apx.3% of total
draw steam.)
12. Every after 12-hour Soot blower should be operated.
13. Water quality checking every after 2 hours – Feed water and Boiler water and as per
requirement Chemical should dose.
14. Maintain Boiler log book.
12.7. Startup check lists enclosed at the end of Book:

content:
13.1. Steps / Sequence of activities for start-up after T&I:
The following activities to be done for Start - up of the Unit / Equipment T&I
i. Receipt of start-up clearance forms from Other sections (Annexure-II)
ii. Checking the all the points mentioned in Start-up check list (Annexure-I)
iii. De-blinding of piping and Equipment
iv. Leak checking and identifying the problem areas
v. Checking of Trips / Interlocks and other critical control schemes / instruments
vi. Utilities commissioning
vii. Procedure for start-up of the plant
13.2. Details of each step of activities for start-up after T&I:
All start up activities after T&I are explained below
13.2.1. Receipt of start-up clearance forms from Other sections.
Ensure receipt of start-up clearance forms from other sections as per Annexure-II
available at the end of Operating Manual.
13.2.2. Checking the all the points mentioned in Start-up check list (Annexure-I):
Check all points mentioned in start-up check list as per Annexure-I available at the end
of Operating Manual.
13.2.3. De-blinding of piping and Equipment:
De-blind the Equipment / Pipe lines and provide gaskets as per the blind list provided
in chapter-28.
13.2.4. Leak checking and identifying the problem areas:
After T&I completion, Vessel or tank to be refilled and leaks if any to be checked and
leaks to be observed through flanges of lines joining the vessel.
13.2.5. Checking of Trips / Interlocks and other critical control schemes / instruments:
1. After T&I completion, the following checkups to be carried out and Equipment to be
restarted as per Normal operation procedure mentioned in Chapter-14
2. Boilers trip checks to be carried out and to be recorded in trip check register.
3. Boiler Live test to be carried out and pop & reset pressure value to be noted in RV
check register.
4. Dearator low & high level alarms to be checked.
5. Auto operation of all MOV (Motor operated Valves) to be checked.
13.2.6. Utilities commissioning:
1. Instrument airline, plant Air lines to be flushed thoroughly and charged and ensure that
the required pressure of available in the lines
2. Service water lines to be charged
13.2.7. Detailed procedure for Boiler start up:
1. Take clearance from all the concerned departments.
2. Inform all concerned including RSM regarding start-up activity.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:13 Rev. No.2 Page 99 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 6 Start Boilers as per procedure. > 13.2 Details of each step of activities for start-up after T&I: > 13.2.7 Detailed procedure for Boiler start up:

content:
3. Confirm furnace man ways are in close position.
4. Confirm that all blinds are removed and entered in the register.
5. All foreign material should be removed from the Boiler area.
6. Ensure that both oil burners are properly fixed and with start-up tip.
7. Drum vents to be kept in open position.
8. Start-up vent to be kept in open position.
9. Both the gauge glasses to be kept in service condition.
10. Super heater inlet and outlet drains to be kept in open position.
11. All the instruments take off valves to be kept in open position.
12. All trip switches to be kept in position.
13. Carry out stroke check of all control valves and ensure that all are in working
condition.
14. Blow down valve to be kept in close position.
15. Feed water to be routed through preheater and Boiler to be filled with Boiler feed
water.
16. The water level in the steam drum to be kept at operating level.
17. Ensure that the Economizer outlet (Stack) damper is in open position.
18. Take clearance from CPP to start the FD Fan motor.
19. FD fan Motor to turbine and turbine to motor auto cut in systems to be checked.
20. Start FD fan turbine as per procedure and keep the turbine at minimum operating
RPM. i.e at 3200 RPM.
21. Keep Motor driven FD fan in Auto in field as well as in MOI.
22. Switch ON the power to igniters.
23. Establish both burners’ pilot flames.
24. Establish fuel oil recirculation and bring fuel oil temperature to more than 100
deg C.
25. Start Bottom gas burner or oil burner.
26. Check the flame pattern and adjust the gas flow.
27. Confirm drum level transmitters are matching with gauge glasses.
28. Maintain drum level at operating condition by giving blow down as and when
required.
29. Steam temperature raise should be limited to 55 deg C per hour.
30. Close the drum vents when the drum pressure reaches 2 kg/cm2.
31. Close the super heater inlet drain when drum pressure reaches 4 kg/cm2.
32. TSP dosing is to be introduced.
33. Keep all the drains on MSL header in open position and throttle when dry steam
is coming out.
34. Increase the firing rate on bottom burner.
35. Keep top gas or oil burner also in to service.
36. Open the main stop valve before drum pressure reaching line pressure (36.5
Kg/Cm2).
37. Open the MSL MOV after Main steam line temperature reached to operating
temperature (365 deg C).
38. Take de super heater control valve in to service.
39. Close the Start-up vent.
40. Close the Super heater outlet drain.
41. Take feed water control in auto.
42. According to the load requirement keep the standby oil/gas burners in service.
43. Maintain outlet O2 in between 2 to 3 %.
44. All the above activities are to be carried out in coordination with MOI supervisor.
13.2.8. WHRS Startup Procedure after T&I
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:13 Rev. No.2 Page 100 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 20 Start FD fan turbine as per procedure and keep the turbine at minimum operating > 13.2.8 WHRS Startup Procedure after T&I

content:
Turn around inspection is the inspection to be carried about as per Schedule fixed in T3C
(Turnaround Time Table) Schedule. It will be done annually. Inspection crew thoroughly
check the WHRS and release IWL (Inspection Work list) which contains all repair to be
done maintenance crew during T&I. After completion of all repairs on WHRS, it is to be
started as per procedure mentioned in the following steps.
a. Receipt of start-up clearance forms from Other sections
b. Checking the all the points mentioned in Start-up check list (Annexure-I)
c. De-blinding of piping and Equipment
d. Leak checking and identifying the problem areas
e. Checking of Trips / Interlocks and other critical control schemes / instruments
f. Utilities commissioning
g. Procedure for start-up of the plant
h. WHRS Startup Sequence after T&I is as follows
1. Ensure Economizer outlet damper is in open position
2. Ensure Feed water quality is good.
3. All air vent and Start up vent partially open and Drain valve closed.
4. Fill water in the Boiler up to 50 percent of Steam Drum.
5. Start CO Boiler by closing new boiler inlet damper and open damper towards
Chimney’
6. Open the Boiler inlet damper partially instead of slowly heat up boiler.
7. After riches 2 Kg/cm²(g) pressure all air vent closed except Startup vent.
8. After rise 5 Kg/cm²(g) crack leak Steam stop valve.
9. Steam line drain valve open.
10. After rise pressure 9 Kg/cm²(g) Steam stop valve to be open and start up vent
and all vent to be closed.
11. Then adjust – Boiler inlet damper & damper towards chimney.
12. Start Feed Water Pump and maintain Normal Water level of steam drum.
13. Every after 4 hours 1-minute main blow down valve should be opened
(Apx.3% of total draw steam.)
14. Every after 12-hour Soot blower should be operated.
15. Water quality checking every after 2 hours – Feed water and Boiler water and
as per requirement Chemical should dose.
16. Maintain Boiler log book.
13.2.9. Sequence of operation for VHP steam header charging:
1. Take clearance from Projects after completion of DHT VHP steam header hook up.
2. Take clearance from Maintenance after completion of the steam controller LSE
clamp.
3. Take clearance from RSM & MOI for charging the VHP steam header at IBH
battery limit.
4. Operate VHP battery limit isolation valve equalizing valve slowly for charging
towards IBH boilers VHP steam header.
5. Bleed the condensate in VHP steam header completely through drain bypass valves
and pressurize the header slowly.
6. Check the header leaks if any at repaired locations.
7. Compare the header instruments from field to MOI DCS.
8. Start IBH Boiler-9 with normal start up procedure and bring the boiler into service.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:13 Rev. No.2 Page 101 of 251

content:
9. Keep VHP BFW turbine pump at IBH and stop 140-PT-01B turbine pump at PP-2.
10. Balance the steam header pressures and stop Wil-8 Boiler slowly.

(Economizer outlet damper/stack inlet damper)
is in open position
2. Ensure all trips are in service
3. Ensure pilot flame is established before flashing
the Oil or gas burner

14.2. Normal operation of Boilers:
14.2.1. Steam management when one Boiler trips (Steam Control Procedure)
Normally FCCU-1 (New) CO Boiler, FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boilers & FCCU-I CO
Boiler-9 or 10 will be in service when complete steam generation from Units (76 T/HR)
and CPP (180 T/HR) is available. Depending on steam demand like maintenance/IBR
inspection of one boiler/HRSG or Unit start-up/shut down standby boilers at PP-1
(WIL-8 or BHPV) will be put on line. One standby boiler will be there always for
emergency use.
Average loads of UTILITIES:FCCU-1 (New) CO Boiler: 35TPH
FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boiler:25TPH
FCCU-I CO Boiler-9 or-10:48TPH
Average loads of CPP: HRSG-I or II:24TPH
HRSG-III:52TPH

In case of IBH-9 or 10 Boiler trip (Deficit 48 T/hr), steam loads can be balanced in the
following way
Increase load on FCCU-1 (New) CO boiler. 07 T/hr
Increase load on FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boiler. 20 T/hr
Reduce steam to De aerators momentarily. 10 T/hr
Change turbine to motor in Units. 8T/hr
Reduce steam to tank form. 3 T/hr
Total 48 T/hr
Tripped Boiler can be restarted after the fault finding, corrective action and temporary
measures taken. In case of delay in restarting standby boilers like IBH-9, WIL-8 or
BHPV Boilers can be started, if required.
Scenario-II
In case of FCCU-1 (New) CO Boiler CO gas diverted to stack then boiler will be
sustained with a load of 35 T/hr with auxiliary firing of AB-1, 2 & 3 FO & FG fires.
Diverted CO can be taken back after the fault finding, corrective action and temporary
measures taken. In case of delay in taking CO like FCCU-1 failure, then standby boilers
like IBH-9, WIL-8 or BHPV Boilers can be started, if required.
Scenario-III
In case of FCCU-1 (New) CO Boiler trip (Deficit 35 T/hr), steam loads can be balanced
in the following way
Increase load on IBH-10 boiler. 20 T/hr
Increase load on FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boiler. 15T/hr
Total 35 T/hr
FCCU-1 (New) CO boiler can be taken back in service after the fault finding, corrective
action and temporary measures taken. In case of delay in taking boiler online, then
standby boilers like IBH-9, WIL-8 or BHPV Boilers can be started, if required.
Scenario-IV
In case of FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boiler CO gas diverted to stack (Deficit 10 T/hr), steam
loads can be balanced in the following way
Increase load on IBH-10 Boiler. 10 T/hr
Diverted CO can be taken back after the fault finding, corrective action and temporary
measures taken. In case of delay in taking CO like FCCU-2 unit failure, then standby
boilers like IBH-9, WIL-8 or BHPV Boilers can be started, if required.
Scenario-V
In case of FCCU-2 (Old) CO Boiler trip (Deficit 25 T/hr), steam loads can be balanced
in the following way
Increase load on IBH-10 boiler. 20 T/hr
Increase load on FCCU-1 (New) CO 05 T/hr
Boiler.
Total 25 T/hr
FCCU-2 (Old) CO boiler can be taken back in service after the fault finding, corrective

standby boilers like IBH-9, WIL-8 or BHPV Boilers can be started, if required.

14.2.2. OCO Boiler CO gas introduction procedure
The following Procedure to be adopted for CO Gas introduction into Old Co Boiler
1. Clearance should be obtained from YSF & Section head for CO gas introduction.
2. Field boiler flue gas path related ducts, pipe line flanges; shut-off valves working
condition shall be pre-checked and shall be completed without pending.
3. Important instruments (CO GAS concentration indicator) and CO gas inlet duct
4. Pressure transmitters shall be fixed for continuous controlling and monitoring.
5. Effective Co-ordination with the FCCU-II(R) personnel for smooth introduction.
DCS Operational personnel action plan:-
1. CO Boiler combustion of CO GAS temperature shall be maintained at 750 deg C
above, to meet complete combustion of CO gas.
2. Slowly bring down the drum level 40% of its normal working level (50%) by
reducing set value of LR403, which is in AUTO MODE (Water in drum will
expand suddenly & drum level increase).
3. Slowly minimize the Boiler steam load by reduce the fuel gas firing rate by closing
the fuel gas control valve (FR426) of both the gas burners to control the steam
generation (pressure control).
4. All steam header pressures shall be maintained at low profile, to accommodate
sudden increase steam load & avoid popping of any PSVs.
5. Give clearance to FCCU-2 MOI Engineer for operation of FCCU-2 CO Boiler inlet
duct pendulum valve towards Boiler.
6. Combustion air control (FRC403) shall be adjusted accordingly, to maintain the
oxygen level & temperature of the flue gas of the stack.
7. After introduction CO GAS, the boiler load shall be balanced with other boilers and
all steam headers shall be normalized back. 8. Information should be given to
Section Head and YSF after completion.
14.2.3. NCO Boiler CO gas introduction procedure
1) To take CO in to the boiler the following 3 conditions should met.
a. HGV (04-SDV-057) is to be in open position.
b. CO Chamber Temperature is to be maintained more than 800 deg C (TSL-4305) with
the help of support burner.
c. VGV (04-SDV-056) is to be in open position.
2) At any case CO chamber Temperature should not exceed 1050 deg C.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:14 Rev. No.2 Page 110 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 8 Start IBH Boiler-9 with normal start up procedure and bring the boiler into service. > 14.2 Normal operation of Boilers: > 14.2.3 NCO Boiler CO gas introduction procedure

content:
3) CO furnace temperature is to be raised to 1000 deg C.
4) Oxygen analyzer (OAIC-4302) is to be taken on manual mode and combustion air flow to
CO burner is to be kept maximum.
5) To compensate the Temperature drop in CO furnace support burner fuel flow to be
increased and CO furnace temperature is to be maintained between 850-900 deg C.
6) When CO furnace temperature is steady between 850-900 deg C, CO gas to be introduced
to the CO furnace by diverting two port slide valve (TPSV) from Stack to CO furnace.
7) As soon as CO is introduced in to the CO furnace, its temperature may go up to 1000 deg
C. Immediately
8) Support burner firing rate is to be reduced to minimum to keep CO furnace temperature in
between 850 to 950 deg C.
9) Blow down to be operated to maintain drum level between 55–65 %.
10) CO furnace air flow is to be adjusted to keep Oxygen analyser (OAIC-43020) reading
between 1 to 1.5 %.
11) CO analyser (COAI-4302) reading is to be in the range of 0 to 200 PPM.
12) In case CO furnace temperature is high and/or CO reading is high CO furnace outlet O2
(OAIC) set point to be increased further.
13) AB-2 to be kept in service with minimum fuel flows to get the required load.
14) GRE outlet flue gas temperature is to be maintained in between 180 and 200 deg C to avoid
due point corrosion.
CO Burner Normal Shut down Procedure:
1) CO gas to be bypassed by diverting the TPSV opening to the stack.
2) CO duct to be purged by opening the air purge line upstream of VGV (04-SDV-056).
3) VGV (04-SDV-056) to be closed with hydraulic motor.
4) Air flow to CO burners to be minimized.
14.2.4. NCO Boiler FO Recirculation
Fuel oil circulation is to be maintained to avoid chocking of fuel oil lines and to have
free flow of fuel oil at various flow rate demands. Keeping fuel oil circulation from tanks
to user points such as burner even when the boiler is not in service is done with a
procedure to avoid the pressure fluctuations in the fuel oil network.
The following procedure to be adopted for establishing FO Recirculation in NCO Boiler
1. MOI SIC will increase the flow of FO through FIC4201 to NCO burners, so
pressure PI4207 at burners will increases.
2. After intimating to MOI slowly open the recirculation block valve at downstream
of FO control valve FIC 4201. By conforming there is no passing of control valve
as well as no pressure drop (PI4207) at burner’s platform.
3. This activity is to be carried out with care as the opening rate of recirculation block
valve will not affect the recirculation flow FI4203 & FO header pressure PI4207 at
burner’s platform.
4. Once recirculation flow is observed (FT 4203), inform to MOI as well as IFO &
wait for few minutes for stability of recirculation flow.
5. Once recirculation established, wait for the stabilization of fuel oil header pressure.
Power Plant-2 Operating Manual Chapter No:14 Rev. No.2 Page 111 of 251
breadcrumb: Actual Photos of various components of manway cover of Steam Drum of Old Co Boiler > 8 Start IBH Boiler-9 with normal start up procedure and bring the boiler into service. > 14.2 Normal operation of Boilers: > 14.2.4 NCO Boiler FO Recirculation

content:
6. Slowly establish the required quantity of FO recirculation with Coordination of
MOI as well as IFO crew.
7. This operation needs Willkie talkie because MOI PA available at north of the
boiler & activity will takes place at south of boiler.
8. Any emergency in MOI, FO recirculation activity should be stopped.
9. Finally inform status of field to MOI.
14.2.5. NCO Boiler (Horizontal Goggle Valve) HGV Opening and Closing
NCO BOILER operation is directly connected to FCCU-1(R) operation as boiler is at the
downstream of FCCU-(R) and the operation of Horizontal Goggle valve straight away
effect the FCCU-1(R). Specified procedure is to be followed while operating HGV.
The following procedure to be adopted for Opening and closing of HGV in NCO Boiler
in NCO Boiler
HGV Valve Opening:
1. Initiate Push Button on the Hydraulic Control Panel for Starting the hydraulic Power
Pack Motor.
2. After the start of the Hydraulic Motor, initiate Push Button “VALVE OPEN” on the
Control Panel.
{{De-Clamping of the Valve Seat to take place}}
{{ De-clamped Limit Switch activation to the Start the Actuator Motor to travel /
move the Disc Frame for Opening (i.e from “CLOSE Position” to “OPEN
Position”}}
{{OPEN LIMIT switch activation to give signal to STOP activator Motor.}}
{{Also, the OPEN LIMIT SWITCH activation, to cause Cylinders to CLAMP
Position.}}
3. Hydraulic Power Pack Motor to be stopped.
Opening of the Horizontal Goggle Valve:
1. De-clamping of the Seat by 8 Power cylinders via Hydraulic Power Pack
2. Travel movement of the Disc Frame – by Electric Actuator & Gear Box &
Electric Control Panel. (Manual Mode Clutch Lever Disengages after resuming
of the Power Supply.)
Clamping of the Seats by Hydraulic Cylinders automatically after disc travel to
either OPEN or CLOSE:
HGV Valve Closing:
1. Initiate Push Button on the Hydraulic Control Panel for Starting the hydraulic
Power Pack Motor.
2. After the start of the Hydraulic Motor, initiate Push Button “VALVE CLOSE”
on the Control Panel.
{{Through a hand operated Lever, the Hydraulic pressure could also be
generated}}
{{De-Clamping of the Valve Seat to take place}}
{{De-clamped Limit Switch signal to the Start the Actuator Motor to travel /
move the Disc Frame for closing (i.e. from “OPEN Position” to “CLOSE
Position”)}}
{{CLOSE LIMIT switch to give signal to STOP activator Motor.}}
{{Also, the CLOSE LIMIT SWITCH activation, to move Cylinders to CLAMP
Position}}

14.2.6. NCO Boiler (Vertical Goggle Valve) HGV Opening and Closing
FCCU-I regenerator outlet flue gas is routed to New CO Boiler for Heat transfer to
take place and Boiler O/L sent to FGD-I for further treatment. NCO BOILER
operation is directly connected to FCCU-1(R) operation as boiler is at the
downstream of FCCU-I. At the D/S of TPSV, New CO Boiler CO inlet and outlet
ducts are having google valves for positive isolation. VGV (Vertical goggle valve)
is located on flue gas inlet duct and HGV (Horizontal goggle valve) located on flue
gas outlet duct. Specified procedure is to be followed while operating VGV.
14.2.7. Opening and closing of the vertical goggle valve (VGV):
1. VGV Valve Opening:
I. Key Points in Opening of the Vertical Goggle Valve:
1. De-clamping of the Seat by 8 Power cylinders via Hydraulic Power Pack
2. Travel movement of the Disc Frame – by Electric Actuator & Gear Box &
Electric Control Panel. (Manual Mode Clutch Lever Disengages after
resuming of the Power Supply.)
II. Vertical Goggle Valve opening operation:
1. Select the local in Hydraulic oil motor Control panel
2. Initiate ON Push Button on the Hydraulic Control Panel for Starting
the hydraulic Power Pack Motor.
3. Observe the Field pressure gauge of Hydraulic oil motor to develop
90-105kg/cm2.
4. After the confirm Hydraulic Motor pressure, initiate Push Button
“VALVE OPEN” on the VGV Control Panel.
5. After initiating Push Button “VALVE OPEN” on the Control
Panel.

De-Clamping of the Valve Seat to take place. Observe Glowing bulb
will transfer from Clamp to DE clamp on the VGV Control Panel.

De-clamped Limit Switch activation to the Start the Actuator
Motor to travel / move the Disc Frame for Opening (from
“CLOSE Position” to “OPEN Position”). Observe the actuator
motor start bulb will glow and VGV open starts.
 Once VGV disc touches the OPEN POSITION LIMIT
SWITCH, it will give signal to STOP activator Motor.
 Observe on VGV control panel Actuator motor will cut off with valve
open feedback and actuator motor stop bulb will glow

Also, the OPEN LIMIT SWITCH activation, to cause Cylinders to
CLAMP Position.
6. Clamping of the Seats by Hydraulic Cylinders automatically after disc travel to
either OPEN or CLOSE.
7. Initiate OFF Push Button on the hydraulic Power Pack Motor, observe
hydraulic Oil pressure drop and Glowing bulb of DE clamp will transfer to
Clamp on the VGV Control Panel.
III. Vertical Goggle Valve closing operation:

14.2.8. Soot blowing of Boilers:
For long retractable soot blower (LRSB) the following items are to be checked
carefully before initial operation of the blower.
 Clutch on power pack shaft should be engaged,
 Lubrication level on power pack
 Chain tension
 Blowing pressure
a)Initial setting
b) Final setting
 SBV Head air valve (Non-return valve) plug is to be removed
 Direction of carriage movement
 Installation of air vents in power pack
 Interference
a) External interference
b)Internal tube (clearance and alignment)
 Packing tightness

content:
Cleaning of tubes external surface is necessary when increase in boiler exit gas
temperature, increase in pressure loss across the boiler (assuming constant boiler load)
and visual inspection of soot is noticed.
It is important to carry out soot blowing when the boiler is stabilized after the initial
startup. Because during initial startup frequently the burners are to be adjusted and the
fuel firing rate is to be altered. This may result in soot, fuel oil spray deposition and
coke formation on the tubes and at burner throat for some time. To avoid furnace
explosions or puffs these deposits are to be cleared with the help of soot blowing and
burner adjustment. Similarly when a boiler is taking out of service soot blowing is to be
carried out to have clean external surfaces of boiler tubes.
Care must be taken to avoid extending the lance into the boiler without flow of blowing
medium or in subjecting the lance to temperatures in excess of 535°C. If the lance
becomes stalled when partially extended, allow blowing to continue in order to cool the
lance before withdrawing from the boiler. When first placing the blower in service, it is
advisable to extend the lance only a small distance at a time and manually retract it (by
means of the local push button). Assure it is in good mechanical condition and the flow
of blowing medium is established properly.
When the soot blowers are not being used, the main blowing medium valve should
always be closed tightly.
OCO boiler soot blowers operation
1. Prior to starting of soot blowing activity, MOI SE to be informed and clearance to be
obtained.
2. Open one/two threads of steam line for complete draining of condensate.
3. Drain the condensate from soot blowers steam line.
4. Open the steam take off valve of soot blowers.
5. Close the drain valve of soot blowers steam line after ensuring total condensate is
drained.
6. Operate one by one individual soot blower’s push button as per availability list of
soot blowers.
7. 2 nos. of soot blowers are located at each zone. Super heater-1, Super heater-2,
evaporator & economizer zone.
8. Soot blowing to be started form Super heater-1, super heater-2 & economizer
respectively.
NCO boiler soot blowers operation
1. Prior to starting of soot blowing activity, MOI SE to informed and clearance to be
taken.
2. Drain the condensate from soot blowers steam line.
3. Open one/two threads of steam line for complete removal of condensate.
4. Close the drain valve of soot blowers steam line.
5. Open the steam valve as per requirement.
6. Soot blowers panel power to be put in ON mode.
7. All soot blowers should be in home position.
8. Start soot blowers in auto mode.
9. After completion of soot blowing soot blower power push button to be kept in OFF
mode. Inform SE after the activity.
